dependencies.notes: The gateway itself has very few hard dependencies — it's stateless and just proxies. However, it is the front door for everything, so its health impacts all users. When the gateway appears down, check upstreams first — a bad upstream can make the gateway look broken. Cloudflare sits in front of us; if Cloudflare has an incident, we appear down globally even if our infra is fine.

common_issues.title: All API endpoints returning 502 / 503
symptoms: External uptime checks firing from multiple regions
100% of requests failing
Cloudflare dashboard showing origin errors
Customers reporting site is down
likely_causes: Upstream service(s) unhealthy — gateway correctly returning 5xx when upstreams fail (check each upstream before assuming gateway issue)
Gateway pods themselves crashed (less common)
Cloudflare misconfiguration or Cloudflare incident
SSL certificate expired
ALB health check failure causing instance deregistration

steps.action: Check gateway pods are running
steps.note: If pods are healthy, the problem is almost certainly an upstream. If pods are crashing, check logs first.

steps.action: Check which upstreams are healthy
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/upstream-health | jq .
steps.note: This lists all upstream health. UNHEALTHY means the circuit breaker has opened for that upstream. A single unhealthy upstream doesn't cause all 502s — check which endpoints are failing.

steps.action: Check SSL certificate validity
steps.command: echo | openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
steps.note: If cert is expired, everything HTTPS will fail. Renew immediately using the cert renewal runbook (find it on Confluence — search 'TLS renewal'). This has a 14-day warning alert that should have fired.

steps.action: Check Cloudflare status
steps.note: If Cloudflare is having an incident, we can't do much. Monitor their status page. In a true Cloudflare outage, direct traffic to origin is possible but not recommended without security review.

steps.action: Check ALB target group health
steps.note: If targets are draining or unhealthy, the gateway is being deregistered. Check the /health endpoint response time — if it's > 2 seconds, ALB marks it unhealthy. See GW-006.

common_issues.escalation: If more than 50% of endpoints are failing for more than 2 minutes, this is P1. Page platform on-call. Notify #incidents channel with initial assessment.

common_issues.title: Circuit breaker open for an upstream service
symptoms: Specific set of endpoints returning 503 with Retry-After header
Datadog shows 'circuit_breaker_state=OPEN' for a service
Logs contain 'circuit breaker OPEN for <service-name>'
likely_causes: Upstream service is genuinely degraded — circuit breaker working correctly
Circuit breaker threshold too low (failure_threshold=1 has caused false opens before — see INC-4021)
Transient network blip caused spike in errors that tripped the breaker

steps.action: Identify which circuit breaker is open
steps.note: Note the state (OPEN, HALF_OPEN, CLOSED) and the error_count and failure_threshold for each upstream.

steps.action: Check if the upstream is actually unhealthy
steps.note: If the upstream's own health endpoint returns 200, the breaker may have tripped on a transient error. If it returns 5xx or times out, the upstream is genuinely down — fix the upstream, not the breaker.

steps.action: Check failure_threshold configuration
steps.command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .circuit_breaker'
steps.note: failure_threshold should be 5. If it's 1, this is the misconfiguration from INC-4021. Change it to 5 and redeploy. This has been a recurring issue.

steps.action: If upstream is healthy, manually reset the circuit breaker
steps.note: Only do this if you have confirmed the upstream is healthy. The breaker will re-open within seconds if the upstream is still bad.

steps.action: Monitor for 5 minutes after reset
steps.note: If the breaker re-opens, the upstream is still unhealthy. Stop resetting and focus on the upstream.

common_issues.escalation: Circuit breaker open for payments-service is P1 — page both platform and payments on-call. Other services are P2.

common_issues.title: Rate limiting blocking legitimate users
symptoms: Specific users or API keys getting 429 errors
Enterprise customers complaining about throttling
High 429 rate in Datadog without corresponding bot traffic spike
likely_causes: Rate limit threshold too low for the traffic pattern
Rate limiter keying on wrong field (IP instead of API key, or vice versa)
Rate limiter reading from wrong IP header — if using remote_addr instead of X-Real-IP, all traffic looks like it comes from the load balancer IP and gets throttled together
Legitimate enterprise users exceeding standard tier limits

steps.action: Identify who is being rate limited
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep '429' | awk '{print $NF}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
steps.note: This shows what's being rate limited. If everything is from one IP (10.0.0.x), the rate limiter is reading from the wrong header — it's seeing the load balancer IP.

steps.action: Check which IP header the rate limiter is using
steps.command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .rate_limit.ip_header'
steps.note: If it's 'remote_addr', change it. This caused an incident before where bot traffic bypassed rate limiting entirely because all requests appeared to be from the same internal IP.

steps.action: Check rate limit tiers for affected user/API key
steps.note: Shows current tier (standard=100/min, enterprise=2000/min) and current usage. If an enterprise customer is on the standard tier, that's a configuration error — update their tier.

steps.action: Temporarily whitelist an affected API key if urgent
steps.note: This is a hacky fix. Comma-separate multiple keys. Restart required. Use only for genuine emergencies. File a proper fix ticket.

common_issues.escalation: If rate limiting is causing revenue impact (enterprise customers blocked), notify Carol and the account manager for the affected customer.

common_issues.title: CORS errors for specific clients or routes
symptoms: Browser console showing CORS errors
Preflight OPTIONS requests returning incorrect headers
Affects specific browsers (often Safari specifically)
Works from Postman but not from browser
likely_causes: Missing Access-Control-Allow headers on OPTIONS response
Vary: Origin header not set — CDN caching CORS responses incorrectly
New endpoint added without CORS config
Safari has stricter CORS preflight caching than Chrome — a config that works in Chrome may fail in Safari

steps.action: Reproduce the CORS error with curl
steps.note: Check the response headers. You should see Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Vary: Origin.

steps.action: Check gateway CORS configuration
steps.command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .cors'
steps.note: Verify that the failing route is included in the CORS rules. New routes are not automatically CORS-enabled.

steps.action: Add CORS config for missing route
steps.command: Edit gateway-config configmap and add the route to the cors.allowed_routes list. Then: kubectl rollout restart deployment/gateway
steps.note: Rollout restart is zero-downtime but will cause brief latency spike. Do during off-peak if possible. Changes to configmap take effect after pod restart.

steps.action: If Cloudflare is caching CORS responses, purge cache
steps.command: Log into Cloudflare > Caching > Cache Purge > Purge by URL for the affected endpoint
steps.note: Cloudflare should not be caching responses without Vary: Origin. If it is, check the Cloudflare cache rules for that route.

common_issues.escalation: CORS errors are usually P3 unless they're blocking a key user flow. Notify #platform-eng during business hours.

common_issues.title: Logging / observability gaps — missing client IPs or log entries
symptoms: All access logs showing 10.0.0.1 as client IP
Grafana dashboards showing zero or flat metrics
Log aggregation showing gaps in log timeline
likely_causes: Gateway logging using remote_addr instead of X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP
Metrics exporter misconfigured or Prometheus scrape job failing
Fluentd overwhelmed and dropping logs during high-volume periods
Metric name changed in a refactor but Grafana dashboard not updated

steps.action: Check what IP is being logged
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep 'access' | tail -5 | jq '.client_ip'
steps.note: If you see 10.0.0.x consistently, it's the load balancer IP. The log format needs to use $http_x_real_ip or $http_x_forwarded_for.

steps.action: Check Prometheus scrape target for gateway
steps.command: kubectl port-forward deploy/prometheus 9090 & sleep 2 && curl -s localhost:9090/api/v1/targets | jq '.data.activeTargets[] | select(.labels.job=="gateway") | {health, lastScrape}'
steps.note: If health is 'down', Prometheus can't reach the gateway metrics endpoint. Check that /metrics is accessible from within the cluster.

steps.action: Check Fluentd buffer and drop rate
steps.note: Fluentd drops logs when the output buffer is full. During incidents, this can cause significant log gaps. Fluentd buffer was increased after INC-4021 but may still be insufficient under extreme load.

steps.action: Check Grafana dashboard queries against actual metric names
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/metrics | grep -i 'request_duration'
steps.note: Compare metric names from /metrics endpoint against what's in the Grafana query. After a refactor in late May, some metrics were renamed and dashboards were not updated. This has happened more than once.

common_issues.escalation: Observability gaps during an incident are significant — escalate to Carol. Log loss during incidents is a post-mortem finding.

common_issues.title: ALB marking gateway instances as unhealthy
symptoms: Instances being deregistered from ALB
Request errors increasing as capacity drops
ALB access logs showing health check failures
gateway pod is running fine but ALB shows it as unhealthy
likely_causes: /health endpoint is too slow (doing DB check or heavy computation) and exceeds ALB's 2-second timeout
Pod is genuinely overwhelmed and can't respond to health checks fast enough
ALB health check misconfigured (wrong port, wrong path)

steps.action: Check /health endpoint response time
steps.expected: Should be < 0.1 seconds. If > 1 second, the health endpoint is too heavy.
steps.note: If the health endpoint is doing a database query or checking upstream connectivity, it's doing too much. Health check should be shallow — just return 200 if the process is alive.

steps.action: Check ALB health check configuration
steps.command: aws elbv2 describe-target-groups --target-group-arns <arn> | jq '.TargetGroups[0] | {HealthCheckPath, HealthCheckPort, HealthCheckIntervalSeconds, HealthCheckTimeoutSeconds, UnhealthyThresholdCount}'
steps.note: HealthCheckTimeoutSeconds should be >= 5. If it's 2 and our health endpoint takes 3 seconds sometimes, we'll have false failures.

steps.action: Temporarily increase ALB timeout if needed
steps.note: This buys time. The real fix is making the /health endpoint return immediately. There's a config option for shallow health check — check gateway config docs or ask Carol.

steps.action: Switch /health to shallow check
steps.note: If this env var exists, it disables the upstream connectivity checks in /health. Use /health/deep for internal monitoring only. This should have been done already — check why it wasn't.

common_issues.escalation: If instances are being rapidly deregistered and traffic is failing, this is P1. Page platform on-call.

general_health_checks: kubectl get pods -l app=gateway — all pods Running, no restarts
Check Cloudflare dashboard for error rate spikes
Check Datadog: 'Gateway Overview' — request rate, p99, 4xx/5xx rates
kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/status | jq . — all breakers CLOSED
Verify SSL cert expiry: echo | openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
known_gaps: Rate limit whitelist via env var is a hack — no proper admin UI for overrides
CORS config is static — adding a new route requires a config change and pod restart
ALB health check timeout configuration is manual — not in Terraform yet (JIRA PLAT-201)
Log IP header config was fixed but may revert if gateway config is re-applied from stale configmap
Circuit breaker threshold was set correctly after INC-4021 but is not validated in CI — could drift again
notes: The gateway is the first thing customers blame when anything is wrong. Usually it's not the gateway — it's an upstream. Always check upstreams and Cloudflare before assuming gateway issues. The gateway itself is stateless and easy to restart, but do not restart without checking whether it would help first.
